posts.content:
Indianness Is Not a Choice — It’s a Cultural Continuum

In a country like India, culture is not a seasonal accessory—it is the very fabric of everyday life. From the way we greet each other to the symbols we revere, Indianness flows through our homes, workplaces, and public spaces. It is not something that can be switched on or off depending on corporate convenience.
The recent controversy around restricting the placement of Hindu deities raises more than just religious concerns—it questions the place of cultural identity in modern corporate India. For millions, these deities are not mere decorations; they are symbols of faith, discipline, and emotional grounding. Asking individuals to lower them—literally or symbolically—can feel like an erasure of identity.
Corporates often argue neutrality as a shield. But neutrality should not translate into selective discomfort. If diversity and inclusion are to mean anything, they must include the freedom to express one's cultural and spiritual identity without fear or forced compromise.
India’s strength has always been its pluralism. It is a land where multiple faiths, languages, and traditions coexist—not by suppression, but by mutual respect. True professionalism does not demand the abandonment of identity; it thrives when individuals feel respected in their entirety.
The real challenge is not about where a symbol is placed, but how a culture is valued. When Indianness becomes negotiable, we risk losing more than tradition—we risk losing authenticity.
In the end, the question is simple:
Can a nation remain rooted if its people are asked to detach from their roots?
Indianness is not a choice. It is a lived reality.
#Indianness #CulturalIdentity #CorporateEthics #RespectFaith #IndiaFirst #WorkplaceCulture #DiversityAndInclusion #CulturalRespect #ModernIndia #IdentityMatters

posts.content:
EXIM MATTERS – EDITORIAL 41
Trade with Insight

This week, Exim Matters reaches you a day later than usual. We appreciate your patience — and perhaps this delay itself reflects the central theme of this edition: in trade, delays rarely arise from intent, but from execution gaps.
Our lead story examines a critical but often overlooked issue — EPCG delays after licence approval. On paper, approvals are faster, digital, and efficient. In practice, however, the process does not end with licence issuance. Where Bank Guarantees are required, execution becomes a multi-layered coordination exercise between importer, bank, and Customs. Format mismatches, internal bank workflows, and confirmation requirements can easily add several days of delay. The insight is clear: in today’s trade environment, efficiency is no longer defined by approvals alone, but by the precision of last-mile execution.
This theme of practical friction versus theoretical clarity runs through the rest of the edition.
The discussion on the Kelly’s impasse prompts us to question whether certain legacy positions continue to serve present-day trade realities. Our piece on GST risks for landlords highlights how compliance failures, even indirect ones, can have unintended consequences. The principle of lex non cogit ad impossibilia reminds us that law must remain grounded in practical feasibility.
On a broader canvas, we celebrate India’s everyday excellence, where quiet consistency continues to build global credibility. And in a surprising but telling example, the story of the USA importing cow dung from India illustrates how even the most unexpected products can find global relevance when positioned correctly.
Together, these perspectives reinforce a simple but important truth:
In trade, success lies not just in policy, but in execution — not just in approval, but in alignment. The magazine can be viewed at https://lnkd.in/g2rQgqfa
Editorial Team

Guided by experience. Shared for the trade community.

posts.content:
The more experience, the better the compliance. Right?

Wrong.

In India's First Independent MFD Risk Profiling Study, we found something that challenges a deeply held assumption:

Average compliance score by experience:
→ Less than 2 years experience: Score 32.0
→ 2-5 years experience: Score 31.6
→ 5-10 years experience: Score 27.7
→ More than 15 years experience: Score 29.1
→ 10-15 years experience: Score 21.3 (LOWEST of all groups)

The most experienced cohort scores the lowest. Why?

Because long-standing habits are the hardest to change. Because "this is how I've always done it" becomes the default answer. Because informal processes that worked for years do not meet SEBI's March 2026 documentation requirements.

Newer MFDs are building compliance habits from scratch — the right way.
Experienced MFDs are carrying the weight of habits built before the new requirements existed.

Compliance is not about how long you've been doing this. It is about whether your process matches what SEBI requires today.

Where does your process stand? Participate in the Study use the link in first comment below 👇

₹𝟮𝟳.𝟯𝟯 𝗖𝗿.

That's how much we saved our clients in FY 25-26 from bogus investment deals and private lending scams.

Just wrapped the reconciliation for our Due-Diligence engagements. The numbers surprised even me.

Here's what nobody tells you about "𝗲𝗮𝘀𝘆 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆" deals in India:

There's a silent cartel operating across the country. And they don't use one playbook, they use seven.

→ Manipulated credit ratings 
→ Inflated documentation fees 
→ Offshore company formation traps 
→ Cash-to-account conversion fees 
→ Collateral engineered into NPAs 
→ Fabricated legal charges 
→ And a longer list I can't fit here

Every deal looks different on the surface. 

The modus operandi underneath? Almost identical.

Since 2024, I've been investing 8-10 hours every week with my team on forensic audits. Reviewing chats. Emails. Agreements. Background checks. 38 parameters before we sign off on any deal.

Why? Because one missed red flag can wipe out a decade of wealth.

What most founders and investors don't realise: 
These scams don't fail because of bad math. 
They succeed because of good psychology.

The cartel counts on three things:

1.Your urgency
2.Your trust in referrals
3.Your reluctance to "offend" by asking hard questions

A forensic lens changes the entire game.

If you're walking into an investment or financing deal right now, and the returns feel too clean, the paperwork too smooth, the timeline too tight, pause.

Get it reviewed before you wire the money, not after.

DM me or drop a comment if you want a confidential Due-Diligence review. That's what we do at Coinshell.

posts.content:
Not every investor writes the same kind of cheque.
 
Some back you because the numbers work. Some because the market is right. And some, the ones you never forget, back you before any of that is obvious.
 
This round was led by Rainmatter by Zerodha and Turbostart. Their conviction, and what it means for where we're headed, deserves its own telling. But this post is about the others. The ones whose belief came earlier, and in some ways, hit harder.
 
Finvolve didn't just back us once. They came back. A follow-on isn't easy capital- it means you've seen the hard parts up close and still chose to go deeper. That kind of renewed conviction is rare.
 
Mudit Kumar and Jeet Wagh showed up before any of the noise. They saw something early and said yes without waiting for validation.
 
Then came Ideabaaz - where Pawan Jaggi, Anupam Bansal and all the Titans made the largest funding commitment in Indian television history on Zee Tv / ZEE5. The belief kept showing up in different forms.
 
And none of this would have a starting point without India Accelerator. That's where we were in our kindergarten days- figuring out the model, building the first version of the thesis, learning what it meant to actually build. They gave us our first institutional shot when the business was still finding its feet.
 
Different moments. Different tables. Different reasons to say yes.
But here we are.
 
Grateful to every single person who bet on this before it was the obvious bet.

I’ve been going through the recent developments around RBI’s proposed NBFC classification changes, and honestly, this feels like a much-needed reset for the sector.

For a long time, NBFC regulation has been evolving in layers—sometimes creating clarity, sometimes adding complexity. This move seems to simplify things in a practical way.

What stands out to me is the sharper focus on **asset size** as a defining factor. It’s a logical step. The larger the balance sheet, the higher the systemic impact—and naturally, the expectation of tighter governance.

At the same time, there appears to be some breathing space for smaller NBFCs. That’s important. Not every NBFC needs to operate under the same regulatory weight, especially when their scale and risk profile are limited.

From a practitioner’s point of view, I see three clear signals:

• Large NBFCs will need to be even more disciplined on compliance, governance, and risk
• Mid-sized players should start preparing today for where they want to be tomorrow
• Smaller NBFCs finally get a chance to focus more on business and less on regulatory overload

Overall, this feels less like a regulatory change and more like a **directional shift**.

RBI is clearly saying — grow, but grow responsibly.

And in today’s environment, that’s exactly what the sector needs.

#NBFC #RBI #Finance #FinancialServices #IndiaEconomy #Compliance

posts.content:
Form-Fatigue No More? 

MCA Proposes a Radical Overhaul of Incorporation Rules 🚀

The Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) has just released the draft Companies (Incorporation) Amendment Rules, 2026, and it’s a significant leap for the #EaseOfDoingBusiness in India. 

For those of us navigating the complexities of the Companies Act, this isn't just a procedural update—it’s a structural shift.

The "Power of Two": E-CHNG and E-CON
The most striking change is the consolidation of nine legacy forms into just two modular e-forms.

Form E-CHNG: A single "Identity & Location" form replacing INC-22, INC-23, and INC-24. Whether you’re shifting your registered office or changing the company name, you’ll now use specific parts of this one form.

Form E-CON: A "Conversion & Approval" powerhouse that merges INC-6, 12, 18, 20, 27, and 28. This streamlines the process for converting entity types and seeking Regional Director approvals.

Beyond Form Consolidation, three updates stand out:

 1. Expanded Boardroom Entry: The cap for applying for Director Identification Numbers (DIN) during incorporation via SPICe+ is increasing from 3 to 5. This is a massive win for larger startups and joint ventures.

 2. Modernizing "Office": The rules finally recognize co-working spaces as valid registered offices, with clearer documentation requirements (like municipal khatas or allotment letters).

 3. Digital First: Official communications are shifting from traditional "Registered Post" to “Speed Post and Email”, aligning the law with 2026's digital reality.

This draft is open for public comment until May 9, 2026. It represents a clear transition from a "filing-heavy" system to a "data-driven" modular ecosystem.

I’d love to hear from my fellow professionals and founders—do you think this consolidation will truly reduce the compliance burden, or will the "modular" nature of these forms bring its own set of complexities?

#CorporateLaw #MCA #Compliance #IndiaBusiness #LegalUpdates #CompaniesAct

Most people associate a family office with billion dollar balance sheets and private investment teams.

That view misses the point.

A family office is not defined by scale. It is defined by structure.

At its core, a family office is a coordinated way to manage wealth, risk, tax planning, succession, and governance. Whether a family has one operating business and a few investments, or a diversified global portfolio, the need for alignment remains the same.

Here is what a family office mindset creates.

- Central visibility across assets instead of fragmented information
- Coordinated tax and estate planning rather than reactive compliance
- Structured investment policy instead of opportunity driven decisions
- Clear governance between family members and business management
- Long term continuity across generations

It does not always mean a large team or a standalone entity. It can begin as a small, disciplined structure with defined reporting, documented policies, and aligned advisors.

Every family with meaningful assets already operates a family office in some form. The question is whether it is intentional or accidental.

posts.content:
Compliance is one of the most underestimated frontiers of innovation in financial services.

When AI in banking is discussed, the spotlight usually goes to chatbots, personalization, or productivity. But one of the most powerful use cases sits in a far more critical function: KYC, screening, and client due diligence.

Compliance teams have carried a heavy operational burden. They screen names across sanctions and PEP lists, investigate adverse media, validate KYC details, review supporting documents, and follow up repeatedly when something is missing or inconsistent. It is essential work, but it is also time-consuming, repetitive, and heavily dependent on manual effort.

This is where AI is starting to make a real difference.

Not by replacing compliance officers.
By helping them operate with more speed, more context, and better precision.

A strong AI-enabled compliance workflow can read unstructured data, compare client information against local and international watchlists, surface inconsistencies in submitted KYC documents, and flag potential issues before submission using historical rejection patterns. That alone can significantly reduce the painful back-and-forth between institutions and clients.

But one of the most valuable use cases goes even further.

AI can support due diligence by scraping publicly available information, connecting scattered digital signals, and helping compliance teams confirm whether a client may actually qualify as a PEP even when that status was not declared. Instead of relying only on direct list matches, the process becomes richer and more investigative, using open-source information to build a more complete risk picture and provide evidence that supports escalation and review.

That shift matters.

The best compliance function is not the one that rejects faster.
It is the one that identifies risk earlier, explains issues more clearly, and helps legitimate clients get it right the first time.

What makes this especially powerful is that it is not only about efficiency. It is about strengthening judgment.

When AI handles the first layer of screening, matching, summarization, public-information research, and document review, compliance professionals can focus on what truly requires human expertise: context, intent, materiality, escalation, and final risk decisions.

The future of compliance is not more people doing more manual checks.
It is better people, augmented by better intelligence, making faster and more reliable decisions.

The institutions that lead the next phase of digital transformation will not be the ones that simply automate workflows. They will be the ones who redesign trust!


#AI #Compliance #KYC #AML #PEP #FinTech #DigitalTransformation #RiskManagement

posts.author_name: Yesterday we kicked off our Compliance panel session at the Antwerp Diamond Bourse to a full house (!), with Kris Meskens (Belgian AML/FIU ), Dave van Moppes (Tuerlinckx) and Thomas Willekens (Panis)… | Karen Rentmeesters
posts.content:
Yesterday we kicked off our Compliance panel session at the Antwerp Diamond Bourse to a full house (!), with Kris Meskens (Belgian AML/FIU ), Dave van Moppes (Tuerlinckx) and Thomas Willekens (Panis) in a conversation moderated by yours truly.
A few takeaways:
Money laundering and terrorist financing are real threats to our industry. They attack our integrity and the trust we need with regulators, financial institutions, clients — with the world. It's frustrating to see the dishonest few taint the honest majority, but that doesn't change the fact that we have a role to play. This is not "just a matter for governments." And let's not forget the human cost, largely invisible to us: behind every laundered dollar is an invisible victim — of human trafficking, drugs, and worse.
Despite what some may hope, this is not going away. The EU's AML directive will continue to level the playing field across the EU next year — important, but it will not make obligations lighter.
Fortunately, the compliance expertise is growing fast, with more specialists available to assist companies in their due diligence.
In addition, technology is making its way into compliance as well, but this too is a process. I believe there is far more opportunity to integrate smart tools, and the idea of a "compliance passport" is definitely worth exploring. The administrative burden should become less and less manual as technology evolves.
Which brings me to a frustration. We sometimes lose track of what we are trying to achieve. Industry, regulators and inspectors alike fall into the trap of making the process the point. What we actually want is for companies to identify and assess risks to the best of their ability, document the steps they take, base transaction decisions on those findings, and report anomalies to the proper authorities.
I get a bit annoyed when I hear that government gauges industry knowledge of AML through questions like whether we know the exact penalty for non-compliance. Does it matter what the exact amount is? Isn't it enough to know the consequences could run a business into the ground? Is knowing someone's home address — understandably sensitive in a high-value environment like ours — really the breaking point in assessing whether a company is doing its job on risk? Could it be that people who genuinely want to file a report are deterred by a user-unfriendly platform?
A little pragmatism goes a long way. That is where AWDC has to play its role — talking with governments and regulators, making sure we don't miss the point. Stay tuned and come see us in the Antwerp Diamond Bourse this week!

posts.content:
AI isn't a cost center. Stop budgeting it like one.
I've managed IT budgets from $3M to $30M across manufacturing, consumer goods, higher education, and life sciences. Four industries, three continents, 25 years.
The single biggest mistake I see companies make with AI?
They put it in the same budget line as infrastructure. That's like putting your sales team under facilities management. It guarantees that AI will be measured by uptime instead of impact — and the first thing cut when margins get tight.
Here's a real example.
As an AI consultant, I work with a client in management advisory, we identified a document processing workflow that was eating 40+ hours per week of manual labor. Enrollment forms, compliance filings, etc— all touched by human hands multiple times before reaching the system of record.
We deployed an AI-driven extraction and classification layer. Not a moonshot. Not a six-month research project. A focused, unglamorous automation that went live in under 60 days.
Annualized savings: six figures. Time redeployed to client-facing work: hundreds of hours per quarter.
That project didn't just pay for itself. It funded the next one.
This is the compounding effect that gets lost when AI lives inside the IT budget. Every AI project should be measured the way you'd measure a business initiative: revenue enabled, cost avoided, margin improved. Not server uptime. Not tickets closed.
I don't greenlight an AI engagement unless the business case shows at least a 3x return on investment. Not because I'm conservative — because anything less than 3x means the project can't survive the first budget review. You need enough margin to absorb the learning curve and still show undeniable value.
PE-backed companies understand this instinctively. They think in EBITDA impact and exit multiples. When I tell a PE operating partner that an AI workflow saved $400K annually on a $120K implementation, they don't see an IT project. They see margin expansion. They see valuation.
That's the language AI needs to speak.
And here's the bridge to something I've been thinking about since I wrote about algorithmic complacency a few weeks ago: the same uncritical acceptance that threatens students also threatens boardrooms. When leadership can't evaluate an AI investment on its merits — because they've never been taught to ask the right questions — they either overspend on hype or underspend on substance. Both are expensive.
If your AI budget lives inside IT, your AI strategy lives inside a cage.
Where does yours sit?

#AI #CIO #EBITDA #DigitalTransformation #ManufacturingAI #HigherEd #FractionalCIO #AIStrategy #AlgorithmicComplacency

posts.author_name:
An SOP only works when people give a damn.

Every operation reaches the same moment. Leadership has done the serious work
posts.content:
An SOP only works when people give a damn.

Every operation reaches the same moment. Leadership has done the serious work: procedures documented, accountability mapped, owners named against every line item. The architecture is coherent, the standards are clear, and something is still missing. 

Structure organises activity, creates consistency, and establishes the minimum below which a team should not fall. In luxury hospitality and wellness, that baseline protects the guest experience, anchors the brand, and gives new team members a coherent place to start. What it cannot do is create the conditions in which people want to perform beyond the minimum.

Transactional management answers what must happen, who owns it, and what failure looks like; those are necessary questions, but no procedure can encode whether the person reading it cares about the outcome. Caring is the precondition for judgment, and judgment is what the document cannot anticipate.

We can spend months refining SOPs, naming owners, and tightening controls; that work creates structure and establishes a baseline, but it determines nothing about what the team does inside the space those structures define. 

If the team carries no sense of ownership, no safety to act, no belief that their judgment will be supported, the system never moves beyond compliance. An SOP only works when people give a damn, and without that, it is just paper managing paper.

I have watched teams follow every step in a protocol and still deliver a flat experience; I have also watched teams who understood the intent rather than merely the instruction read a guest mid-interaction and adjust in real time to create something that mattered. The determining variable was never the quality of the procedure; it was whether the individual believed their judgment was trusted, their contribution counted, and their ownership of the outcome genuinely meaningful.

That belief comes from how leaders behave, most acutely in the moments when someone takes a call the manual never anticipated. Whether people feel safe to act, know the outcome not just the steps, and trust that their judgment will be backed. These are not soft questions; they determine whether a team performs or merely complies.

Senior leaders reach for structure when performance drops; more often, the problem is not an absent procedure but a team with no investment in the outcome and no reason to develop one.

Transformational leadership creates the conditions for people to think, own the result, and extend their discretion into the space the document could not anticipate; what leaders do with trust, presence, and accountability determines everything above the floor that structure sets.

Build the system, then build the people who run it; the second task is harder, and it is the only one that compounds.

... ... 

Ely Cathedral: 300 years to build. Not one person who started it lived to see it finished.

because cost is created when decisions have consequences, not when budgets get approved.

Projects make cost look clean.

✅ Budget approved
✅ Tool purchased

posts.content:
The real cost is always delayed
because cost is created when decisions have consequences, not when budgets get approved.

Projects make cost look clean.

✅ Budget approved
✅ Tool purchased
✅ System deployed
✅ Spend recorded

That is convenient. It is also incomplete.

Accounting captures a point in time.

It does not capture the trajectory that follows.

This gap becomes sharper with AI.

An AI system can look cheap to deploy.

→ Scalable infrastructure
→ Accessible tooling
→ Shorter delivery cycles

So the initial investment feels controlled. The real cost shows up later.

It emerges when decisions accumulate.

→ Edge cases handled incorrectly but consistently.

→ Acceptable classifications start breaking downstream processes.

→ Plausible outputs shape actions at scale.

Each instance looks minor. Together, they become outcomes.

That is where cost changes form.

It stops being a transaction.

It becomes embedded in:

✅ Rework
✅ Corrective actions
✅ Exceptions that keep returning
✅ Manual interventions reappearing in new places
✅ Compliance adjustments after the fact

Early signals can even look positive.

→ Throughput rises
→ Cycle times drop
→ Dashboards show improvement

Then a different pattern appears.

→ Corrections increase
→ Exceptions accumulate
→ Workarounds return

By then, cost is no longer a line item.

It is a distributed pattern that is hard to isolate.

That is why implementation is rarely the moment of highest risk.

It is the moment of lowest visibility.

The system is judged by what it cost to build.

Its impact is defined by what it costs to live with.

Those are not the same.

So monitoring does not end at deployment.

Monitoring begins at deployment, because the biggest costs are delayed, cumulative, and often indirect. | 11 comments on LinkedIn

🚀 Is your traditional MDM holding back your AI ambitions? 🚀

Traditional Master Data Management (MDM) often gets stuck in rigid rules and manual processes. In the age of AI, this just won't cut it. Your business needs an agile, intelligent data foundation to thrive.

Let's look at how AI is transforming MDM into a dynamic powerhouse, shifting from rule-bound to results-driven:

✨ A New Perspective on MDM Implementation: The AI Advantage
1️⃣ 🧠 Intelligent Data Governance Utilize AI for automated policy enforcement, dynamic compliance monitoring, and continuous rule discovery. No more playing catch-up with shifting regulations.

2️⃣ 🤝 Smart Matching & Entity Resolution Deploy ML models that learn and adapt over time, handling complex data variations, misspellings, and context with higher accuracy and significantly reduced manual effort.

3️⃣ 🧑‍💻 Augmented Data Stewardship Shift your team from routine, manual data corrections to high-value strategic decisions. AI assistants provide proactive alerts and remediation guidance, streamlining their workflow.

4️⃣ 🌐 Dynamic Data Modeling & Semantics Build flexible data structures that truly understand meaning, context, and relationships. This approach discovers new attributes and supports evolving business needs in real-time.

5️⃣ 🛑 Predictive & Proactive Data Quality Anticipate and prevent poor data ingestion before it impacts downstream analytics. ML-driven anomaly detection and self-healing pipelines ensure clean, reliable data.

6️⃣ ⚡ Rapid Time-to-Value & Continuous Enrichment Get faster insights with accelerated deployment cycles. Continuously enrich your master data from automated third-party sources, making your data an asset rather than a project.

Ready to transition your MDM from an operational headache to a strategic AI advantage? The answer lies in leveraging the very intelligence you're trying to build. Move beyond the spreadsheet mentality to an intelligent data ecosystem.

$15 trillion moves through accounts receivable every year, mostly managed through manual emails and spreadsheets. That gap will not last much longer.

Most finance teams know AR matters for cash flow. Fewer treat it with the same rigor they apply to other parts of the balance sheet.

• 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗽𝗿𝗮𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀: Proactive outreach at the right moment dramatically changes collection response rates

posts.content:
99% of Indian founders get this wrong before they even launch. 

I've worked with 200+ startups across India.

Here's what kills most of them....before they even get their first customer:

THE 4 INVISIBLE LANDMINES ↓

1. Wrong company structure from Day 1
Pvt Ltd? LLP? OPC? Most founders pick randomly. This one decision affects your funding eligibility, tax liability, and exit options....forever.

2. ESOP not set up before hiring
You promise equity to your first 5 employees. No ESOP pool. No vesting schedule. No documentation. Suddenly you have a co-founder dispute....on someone you never even made a co-founder.


3. FEMA violations no one talks about
You get your first foreign investment. You celebrate. Then....6 months later....a notice arrives. Because nobody filed the FC-GPR. This shuts deals and reputations.


4. Stock audit skipped at scale
Growing fast? Great. But your cap table is a mess, your shareholding hasn't been audited, and Series A investors are finding discrepancies. That's a red flag that kills rounds.

posts.content:
ESG reporting is expanding fast. ESG assurance capability isn’t keeping up, especially in India. And that gap is about to become visible.

Globally, ESG assurance is no longer treated as a sustainability exercise. Regulators and investors now view it as financial-grade information that must withstand the same scrutiny as audited numbers.

What global expectations now look like:
• Verifiable data trails, not narrative disclosures.
• Clear methodologies behind ESG metrics.
• Strong internal controls over non-financial data.
• Independence and assurance frameworks comparable to audit.

In many cases, ESG assurance is moving from limited assurance toward audit-level rigour.

𝐖𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐚𝐧 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐝𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 𝐢𝐬 𝐥𝐚𝐠𝐠𝐢𝐧𝐠
Many organisations still treat ESG as a reporting project led by sustainability teams, while assurance is expected to validate outputs late in the cycle. That approach doesn’t work anymore. 

Common gaps:
• ESG data collected without control environments.
• Metrics defined inconsistently across periods.
• Technology systems not built for auditability.
• Assurance teams brought in after disclosures are drafted.

You can’t assure what wasn’t designed to be assured.

𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐦𝐞𝐚𝐧𝐬 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐂𝐀 𝐟𝐢𝐫𝐦𝐬
ESG assurance isn’t an extension of statutory audit, it’s a new discipline combining accounting, risk, operations, and data governance. 

Firms that succeed will:
• Build multidisciplinary teams.
• Define assurance methodologies early.
• Strengthen independence safeguards.
• Focus on process assurance, not report verification.

The real shift is philosophical.
ESG assurance is moving from 𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐫𝐲 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 to 𝐞𝐯𝐢𝐝𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧.

Global markets are raising expectations faster than domestic ecosystems are preparing for them. Indian firms and companies that invest early will gain credibility and pricing power. Those that wait will find themselves scrambling when assurance becomes mandatory rather than optional.

ESG readiness is no longer about publishing better reports. It’s about building systems that can survive assurance.

#trc #esg #esgassurance #sustainabilityreporting #corporategovernance

If AI is transforming Wealth Management, why does most of the Industry still feel stuck?

That’s the question Sohini Bagchi  at TechCircle put to me last week.

The hard problem in WealthTech isn’t AI. It’s the reporting lag, proprietary data formats, the gap between markets that move in seconds and portfolio reports that move in days. 

Wealth Managers end up as forensic scientists, stitching together fragmented data instead of sitting with their clients. The data is everywhere and useful nowhere.

In the piece I cover three things every WealthTech builder should be thinking about:

→ Data as a Product — moving from messy silos to live, decision-ready data that’s ready the moment it’s needed

→ Architecture over Add-ons — making AI a first-class citizen and embedding governance at the core, not bolted on before the next audit

→ The Unglamorous Win — where AI is actually delivering the most immediate impact right now, and it’s not where most people expect.

I believe that the next wave of winners in Indian WealthTech won’t be the ones with the flashiest models. They’ll be the ones who did the unglamorous work underneath.

At Centricity, we’re working aggressively to build the next gen WealthTech experience with Data & AI integrated as the core layer — a state-of-the-art, multi-product, multi-asset Wealth Management platform where the above points isn’t the vision. It’s the foundation.

Full piece linked in the comments, worth a read if you’re building, advising, or investing in this space.

22 days, 3 unknown countries and…

→ I almost got robbed.
→ I was shit scared driving alone on pitch-dark cliff roads with no street lights.
→ I met people who were rude for no reason.
→ I trekked with strangers at 4 AM.
→ I ran my first 7 km circling an entire island.
→ I did my first scuba dive without knowing how to swim.
→ I didn’t eat Indian food for 22 days.
→ I met people who were genuinely interested in my business.
→ I negotiated prices in currencies I had no idea about.
→ I made last-minute plans that turned into the best memories.
→ I sat alone at beaches just thinking about life.
→ I pushed myself into situations I would’ve avoided back home.

And this is exactly the kind of experience I invest my money in.

Not just for fun.
But for growth.

Because when you put yourself in unknown environments:
→ You become more confident
→ You take faster decisions
→ You handle uncertainty better
→ You understand people better
→ You think beyond your usual limits

And this reflects in your business too.

You stop playing safe.
You stop overthinking small risks.
You start seeing bigger opportunities.

Most people only invest in things they can measure.

Courses. Tools. Assets.

But experiences?

They compound in ways you can’t quantify.

They change how you think.
And how you think changes everything you do.

So yes, this trip had its risks.

But it also gave me something more valuable:

A stronger version of myself.

And that’s always worth the investment.

P.S.: Would you put yourself in uncomfortable situations intentionally? | 96 comments on LinkedIn

Then... we met THE Nithin Kamath ☺️ 
 
I won't pretend I wasn't nervous. But what struck me wasn't his stature. It was how clearly he saw the problem we were solving. Not just as a legal-tech opportunity. As a foundational gap in India's ecosystem. Enforcement had become real-time. Resolution hadn't. And that asymmetry was holding back millions of vehicle owners, drivers, and fleet operators every single day.
 
He got it. Completely.
 
A few days later, Rainmatter's team flew to our office in Gurgaon. They didn't just take a meeting, they spent time with our entire team. Watched how incidents get logged, triaged, and resolved. Saw the system work in real time. Across geographies. Across legal situations.
 
That visit led to their investment decision.
 
And that, for me, says everything about how Rainmatter operates. They don't just back a pitch deck. They back what's actually being built.
 
Getting Zerodha's Rainmatter on board isn't just capital. It's validation that the problem is real, the infrastructure is sound, and the foundation we've been laying quietly for years is worth building on.
 
We've always believed that legal infrastructure in India is broken at a systemic level. It's humbling when someone of Nithin's standing looks at what you're building and says- yes, this is exactly what needs to exist.
 
The journey just got a lot more meaningful.

Pressure Vessels at Sea: DNV vs ABS vs Lloyd’s Register

Pressure vessels are critical components onboard ships and offshore systems—handling compressed air, fuel systems, hydraulics, refrigeration, and steam applications. But what many engineers overlook is this: **their design and approval are not just engineering tasks—they are governed by classification society rules.

In the marine industry, three major classification societies dominate global standards:
DNV, American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), and Lloyd’s Register.

Each brings a slightly different philosophy, but all aim at the same objective: safe, reliable, and compliant pressure systems at sea.

DNV is known for its  risk-based and offshore-focused approach* especially for high-pressure gas systems, fatigue design, and low-temperature toughness. ABS applies a more practical, ASME-aligned framework making it widely used in global shipbuilding with strong emphasis on inspection and documentation. Lloyd’s Register follows a conservative, lifecycle-driven approach with detailed attention to materials, corrosion allowance, and long-term operational integrity.

For marine engineers, this means one thing: ASME alone is not enough. Every pressure vessel must also satisfy additional class rules covering:

* Material traceability and certification
* Welding procedure qualification (WPS/PQR)
* Hydrostatic testing and safety margins
* NDT requirements (RT/UT/MT)
* Safety valve sizing and protection systems

Understanding these differences is essential for design approval, ship construction, and survey compliance.

I’ve summarized the key differences in detail here:

👉 [https://lnkd.in/ghgYsF9B)

In modern marine engineering, compliance is not paperwork—it is the foundation of operational safety and asset integrity.

posts.content:
🚀 Amazing Conversation...with Nitin Sawant around current momentum of Account Aggregator (AA) 

While most conversations focus on UPI, a powerful transformation is quietly reshaping India’s financial ecosystem — Account Aggregator (AA). As of early 2026, India's Account Aggregator (AA) framework has matured into a cornerstone of digital financial infrastructure, with over 17 licensed AAs and 135+ Financial Information Providers (FIPs) enabling secure, consent-based data sharing

Today, AA is becoming core financial infrastructure 
➟180M+ accounts linked
➟ ₹1.4+ lakh crore lending enabled
➟Powering ~10% of retail & MSME credit
➟Largest open finance implementation globally
➟A reference model for emerging markets
➟Part of G20 Digital Public Infrastructure narrative

 🚀 What makes AA game-changing?
➟ Consent-driven data sharing - For the first time, individuals and businesses control their financial data — securely and digitally
➟Faster, smarter lending From days to minutes — cashflow-based underwriting is unlocking credit for MSMEs and new-to-credit customers.
➟Foundation for Open Finance - AA is doing for data what UPI did for payments — creating a scalable, interoperable ecosystem

🎯 Key Challenges (Reality Check) Despite momentum, gaps remain
1. Adoption Gaps
➟  PSU banks & smaller NBFCs not fully integrated
➟  Low consumer awareness

2. Data Challenges
➟ Inconsistent data quality
➟ API standardization issues

3. Use-case Concentration
➟ Heavy dependency on lending
➟ Wealth, insurance still underutilized

🏆 Lending is the Killer Use Case (So Far)  
1. Instant underwriting using 
➟ Bank statements
➟ Cashflows
➟  GST data , Reduces: Fraud , Manual docs and TAT

2. MSME Credit Revolution
➟  Cashflow-based lending
➟  Thin-file borrower inclusion
 
Rise of OMS Fintech Account Aggregator Pvt. Ltd.  

1. Documentation : Physical / PDF Uploads ➟  100% Paperless / Digital
2. Data Verification: Manual (Hours/Days) ➟  Instant (Seconds)
3. Data Security : Low (Email/WhatsApp) ➟  High (End-to-End Encrypted)
4. Underwriting : Asset-Based ➟  Real-Time Cash-Flow Based
5. Approval TAT : 3 to 15 Days ➟  5 Minutes to 2 Hours
6. Fraud Risk : High (Forged Documents) ➟  Zero (Source-Verified Data)
7. User Effort : Very High ➟  Minimal (Single-Tap Consent)

📌 Key Developments in 2026 
➟ Comprehensive Data Access: Beyond bank statements, AA now facilitates the sharing of over 20 types of financial information, including GST data, insurance policies, mutual funds, and pension funds
➟ Cross-Sectoral Integration: SEBI-regulated entities (capital markets) are now fully integrated, allowing for a comprehensive view of a user's financial profile

Let's look at Enhanced Security & Consent The framework operates on a "privacy by design" principle, with new Self-Regulatory Organization (SRO) guidelines focusing on vernacular consent and enhanced security

BLOCKCHAIN IN FINANCIAL INCLUSION
Access to useful and affordable financial products & services

The Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Financial Inclusion Index (FI-Index) for March 2025 rose to 67.0, up from 64.2 in March 2024, reflecting improved access, usage, and quality of financial services. 

📌 India Financial Inclusion at a Glance
➟  67.0 RBI FI Index 
➟  56 crore Jan Dhan accounts
➟  55% women beneficiaries
➟ 68% rural / semi-urban
➟  ₹2.5 lakh crore deposits
➟  85% digital transactions via UPI
➟  142 crore Aadhaar identities

🎯 Key Dimensions of Financial Inclusion
1. Access
o Ability to open and use a financial account 
o Availability of banking or digital channels 

2. Usage
o Regular transactions (not just dormant accounts) 
o Active participation in the financial system 

3. Quality
o Services that meet user needs (affordable, relevant) 
o Tailored products (e.g., farmer loans, micro-insurance) 

4. Affordability
o Low transaction costs 
o No hidden charges 

5. Trust
o Safe, secure &transparent systems

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬

🌎 Blockchain Impact Framework for Financial Inclusion
With all its Immutable , Decentralized nature , Blockchain is perfect fit for key dimensions ... 
 
1. Access (Onboarding the Unbanked)
o Mobile-based onboarding 
o Digital wallets instead of bank accounts 
o Integration with digital identity systems 

2. Usage (From Access to Activity)
o Low-cost transactions Micro-payments & micro-loans become viable 
o Direct peer-to-peer interactions 

3. Quality (Better Financial Products)
o Customized financial products 
o Faster, cheaper, and more efficient services 

4. Trust (Foundation of Inclusion)
o Immutable records 
o Transparent transactions 

▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬▬

◉  Thus Blockchain Enables  
➟ Low-cost transactions
➟ Instant / near real-time settlements
➟ Borderless access
➟ Trust & transparency by design
➟ Interoperability across systems

➟ Cross-Border Payments & Remittances
➟ Digital Identity (Self-Sovereign Identity)

🇮🇳 India Digital Rupee Initiative by Reserve Bank of India - Financial inclusion, Payment efficiency , Reduced cash dependency

🎯 Bottomline
India has moved from financial access to financial empowerment—powered by Jan Dhan, Aadhaar, UPI, and now the next frontier could be blockchain-enabled trust systems.If we only focus on inclusion—opening accounts—we fail. If people don’t understand how to use financial services, they won’t use them. And if there is no grievance redressal, trust breaks—and the system collapses.

posts.content:
Banks Are No Longer Piloting AI Agents. They're Running Them.

For years, banks talked about AI in future tense. "We're exploring it." "We're evaluating use cases." "We're in pilot mode."

That chapter is closing.

2026 is the year banks moved from demos to daily operations with AI agents. Here's what's actually happening — and why it matters for anyone working in finance.

What's changed?

At SIBOS 2025 and Money20/20 Europe, compliance and operations leaders from Tier 1 European and Asian banks shared something notable: agent-based workflows had become one of their most material cost levers. Not in three years. Now.

The agents being deployed aren't general-purpose assistants. They're narrow, purpose-built systems handling specific operational tasks — assembling AML case files, extracting and validating onboarding documents, collecting sanctions evidence, orchestrating system handoffs, and escalating cases with full procedural context.

Once the tedious work of case assembly and evidence preparation got automated, compliance teams stopped reconstructing information and started reviewing it. In production environments, this shift alone has reduced perceived capacity constraints in compliance teams by roughly 30–40%. That's not a pilot number — that's a real operating model change.

The bigger picture

McKinsey's Global Banking Annual Review 2025 found 71% of banking executives said AI would materially reshape their operating models. But the more important insight: the banks winning aren't treating AI as an auxiliary feature. They're embedding it into core architecture.

Oracle frames this as "Banking 4.0" — where AI agents form the default interface for everything from retail onboarding to FX hedging for corporate clients. Contextual AI is replacing keyword-based financial crime detection, cutting through alert noise by reading relationships, transaction behaviour, and entity linkages — not just names.

Meanwhile, McKinsey warns that agentic AI could erode up to $170 billion in global bank profits by 2030 for institutions that fail to adapt. The window to move is narrowing.

───

AI agents in banking aren't a technology story. They're an operational reality story — about efficiency, risk management, and competitive positioning. The question is no longer whether. It's how fast, and who's already ahead of you.

#AI #Banking #DigitalTransformation

Further Reading:

• Oracle: The Future of Banking — Production-Scale AI Agents
• LatentBridge: AI Trends Reshaping Banking Operations, Compliance and Risk
• Anaconda: AI in Banking — How Banks Are Scaling Intelligent Systems
posts.summary: Banks Are No Longer Piloting AI Agents. They're Running Them. For years, banks talked about AI in future tense.

posts.content:
Today, we launched the Unicorn Growth Fund.
INR 6,000cr. Backed by KRAFTON Inc. and NAVER Corp — two of South Korea's largest technology companies, and managed and advised by Mirae Asset Venture Investments. It is one of the largest India-focused technology fund pools ever raised from Asia.
The announcement was made in New Delhi yesterday, on the sidelines of the Korean President's state visit to India — alongside Hon'ble Minister Piyush Goyal, Hon'ble Minister Jung-Kwan Kim, and the global CEOs of KRAFTON and Naver. 
The Fund will back growth-stage Indian founders across four themes:
→ Technology Platforms — consumer internet, marketplaces, next-gen platform infra 
→ Consumer Discretionary — digitally-native brands for India's rising middle class 
→ AI & Software — generative AI, applied AI, enterprise SaaS, developer tooling → Deep Tech — semiconductors, space-tech, robotics, advanced materials

India is at an inflection point. Over the next decade, a new generation of Indian technology champions will be built in India — for India and the world. Those founders will need capital. They will also need product, AI, gaming, and platform expertise from partners who have built at scale globally. That is what our on-the-ground Mirae Asset team in India will activate with expertise from KRAFTON and Naver. 
To the founders, if you are building a category-leading company with global ambition, we would love to hear from you.
Grateful to Changhan Kim, Soo Yeon Choi, Sean (Hyunil) Sohn, the KRAFTON and Naver teams, Hon'ble Minister Piyush Goyal, Hon'ble Minister Jung-Kwan Kim, the Mirae Asset leadership (Swarup Mohanty, Ryan Kim, Ph.D, Taewan Kwon, and Mirae Asset Venture Investments (India) colleagues who made this happen.

Now the real work begins. | 40 comments on LinkedIn

posts.content:
Most family businesses don’t collapse because of competitors.

They collapse when the dinner table becomes the boardroom.

At the Lineage + Capital Session, one theme stood out clearly.
Family businesses are rarely broken by markets. They are broken by emotions without structure.

Sulajja Firodia Motwani shared a difficult truth. When competition overlaps with family relationships, many turn to the senior-most person to resolve conflict.

But enduring businesses cannot rely on constant parental arbitration.
At some point, the next generation must balance ambition with relationships.

Gauri Kirloskar added another powerful perspective. Alignment does not happen automatically just because people share the same surname.

As families grow, visions diverge. Priorities shift. Risk appetite changes.
The families that last design alignment intentionally.
Clear governance. Structured conversations. Defined roles.

Not because conflict exists. But because preventing conflict requires structure.
Manish Chokhani brought in a financial lens.

Many promoter families feel pressure to prove sophistication outside their core business.
But enduring wealth is usually created by focusing on what you understand deeply.

Running great businesses. Staying disciplined. Thinking long term.
The biggest insight from the session was simple.
Family businesses are human systems.

And human systems only last with:
Clarity in roles. 
Structure in decision-making. 
Humility in leadership.

Because the real legacy is not just wealth.

It is whether the family stays intact while building it.
If the next generation stepped into leadership tomorrow,

Would your business run on clear systems or unspoken expectations? | 23 comments on LinkedIn

posts.content:
India’s cola market flips the global narrative.

Globally, PepsiCo is a dominant player.
But in India, Thums Up continues to lead and by a wide margin.

What’s driving this?

• Positioning: Thums Up’s “strong taste” aligns better with Indian preferences, while Pepsi remains youth-centric
• Distribution moat: Backed by Coca-Cola, Thums Up has deeper last-mile reach
• Capital efficiency: Higher market share despite heavy ad spends, better marketing ROI
• Scale economics: 2x daily volumes, stronger operating leverage & brand visibility

What stands out:

This isn’t just a branding battle, it’s a case of execution, localisation, and distribution strength outperforming global brand equity.

In emerging markets, who executes better locally wins, not necessarily who is bigger globally. | 46 comments on LinkedIn
